source_claim: Milk butter calves cattle and bells show abnormal signs before the death-news arrives
Mādhari organises a kuravai to relieve distress affecting the cattle and settlement
Seven young women receive musical-note and dramatic identities in an ordered performance
The dancers enact a pastoral sacred world of Māyavan Balarāma and Piññai
The kuravai praises the Pāṇḍya Cōḻa and Cēra rulers within one Tamil political field
Song and dance are explicitly directed toward removing communal distress and restoring victory
A city cry interrupts the completed kuravai and the accusation against Kōvalaṉ is reported
Kaṇṇaki collapses and repeatedly identifies royal error as the cause of her widowhood
A disembodied voice confirms Kōvalaṉ was not a thief and foretells fire for the city
Kaṇṇaki carries the remaining anklet through Madurai as material evidence and public claim
Citizens interpret her suffering as proof that the supposedly straight sceptre has bent
The city crowd guides Kaṇṇaki to Kōvalaṉ's body and becomes witness to the injustice
Kaṇṇaki embraces and laments Kōvalaṉ's bloodied body in the public street
She asks whether women elders and gods still exist in a city where royal violence goes unanswered
Kaṇṇaki resolves not to rest until she confronts the king and demands an account
